n: Charles Hosmer Morse Museum of American Art
s: World's largest Tiffany collection, rescued from a burning Long Island estate
w: When Tiffany's Laurelton Hall burned in 1957, curator Hugh McKean's wife said: 'Let's buy everything that is left and try to save it.' They did. The chapel he built for the 1893 Chicago World's Fair stands reassembled here, mosaics intact. The most comprehensive Tiffany collection on earth, hiding in plain sight on Park Avenue.
_facts: Founded 1942 by Jeannette Genius McKean, dedicated to her grandfather Chicago industrialist Charles Hosmer Morse
In 1957 McKean learned Tiffany's estate Laurelton Hall had burned; he salvaged major architectural elements
Houses Tiffany's 1893 chapel built for the World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago, reconstructed on-site
Collection includes every medium Tiffany explored — leaded glass, pottery, jewelry, enamels, Favrile blown glass
Opened at current 445 N Park Avenue location in 1995, with 19,000+ sq ft of exhibition space

s: Mayan Revival fantasy compound, a 1937 artist colony now a National Historic Landmark
w: Architect J. Andre Smith built this Maya-meets-Art-Deco complex in 1937 as an experimental artist colony funded by Philadelphia heiress Mary Curtis Bok. Ralston Crawford and Milton Avery painted here. One of the few examples of Mayan Revival architecture in the American South — part studio, part fever dream.
_facts: Founded 1937 by architect and artist Jules Andre Smith (1880-1959) as 'The Research Studio' artist colony
Funded by philanthropist Mary Curtis Bok (later Zimbalist) of the Curtis Publishing family
Hosted artists Ralston Crawford, Milton Avery, and Consuelo Kanaga
Listed on National Register of Historic Places 1982; designated a National Historic Landmark in August 2014
One of the few examples of Mayan Revival architecture (also called Fantasy Architecture) in the southeastern US

s: Arthouse cinema that opened with Lillian Gish watching D.W. Griffith in 1985
w: Founded by Tina Tiedtke in 1985, Enzian launched with a screening of D.W. Griffith's 'Broken Blossoms' — and silent-film legend Lillian Gish was in the audience. Named for a genus of Alpine mountain flower, it hosts the Florida Film Festival and serves food and drinks at your table. One of the finest rep cinemas in the South.
_facts: Founded by Tina Tiedtke, opened February 15, 1985
Opening night featured D.W. Griffith's 'Broken Blossoms' with silent film star Lillian Gish in attendance
Name 'Enzian' is German for gentian, a genus of mountain flower
Nonprofit arthouse theater hosting the Florida Film Festival and other local film festivals
Patrons sit at tables with food and drinks served during films; motto is 'Film Is Art'

s: Former private home on Lake Formosa, now guardian of Earl Cunningham's luminous Florida landscapes
w: Built as the home of Philip Phillips' son Howard, this lakeside house holds the largest collection of self-taught painter Earl Cunningham's glowing Florida twilights anywhere. A 350-year-old live oak called 'The Mayor' lords over the sculpture garden. Donated to the city in 1998, it quietly outlasts every louder neighbour.
_facts: Opened November 1998, established to preserve paintings by self-taught artist Earl Cunningham (1893-1977)
Houses the largest collection of Earl Cunningham paintings in the United States
Building was formerly the private residence of Howard Phillips, son of philanthropist Dr. Philip Phillips
Located in Loch Haven Cultural Park along the shore of Lake Formosa, owned by the City of Orlando
The Marilyn L. Mennello Sculpture Garden features a 350-year-old live oak nicknamed 'The Mayor'

s: First-magnitude spring pouring 42 million gallons a day into Central Florida's oldest canoe run
w: A constant 72 degrees year-round, this first-magnitude spring fed swimming hole and headwaters of the Wekiva River was 'Wekiwa' to the Muscogee Creek — meaning spring. In 1941 an Apopka sportsmen's club bought it from a turpentine company; the state took over in 1970. Four campsites are accessible only by canoe. Old Florida, barely touched.
_facts: Located in Apopka, 20 minutes north of Orlando; 7,000-acre Florida State Park
Spring maintains a year-round water temperature of 72 degrees F, providing about 42 million gallons per day to the Wekiva River
Wekiwa is the Creek language word for a spring; 'Wekiva' spelling for the river came from developers
Apopka Sportsmen's Club purchased from the Wilson Cypress Company in 1941; became state park starting 1970
Four primitive campsites accessible only by canoe or kayak

s: Florida's largest spring on the St. Johns River, winter refuge for hundreds of manatees
w: Botanist John Bartram passed through in 1766. By the 1880s the Thursby family ran a tourist operation from the shell mound above, until a killing freeze wiped out the citrus and drove the trade south. Now the park's 73-degree waters attract hundreds of manatees each winter, their scar patterns tracked in one of the world's longest-running marine mammal databases.
_facts: Largest spring on the St. Johns River; about 102 million US gallons of water flow out daily
Visited by botanist John Bartram in 1766
Thursby family built a plantation-style home on a shell mound in mid-19th century; small railway once linked Orange City to the dock
Acquired by Florida Department of Environmental Protection in 1972 to kick-start its manatee protection program
Manatee researchers have tracked individual animals' life histories by scar patterns since 1978 — one of the world's longest-running marine mammal databases

s: One of the first self-governing all-Black towns in the US, birthplace of Zora Neale Hurston's world
w: Incorporated August 15, 1887, Eatonville was one of the first self-governing all-Black municipalities in America. Zora Neale Hurston grew up on these streets; the town's folklore and characters live in 'Their Eyes Were Watching God.' The Hungerford Vocational School opened here in 1897. Annually the Zora Festival brings it back to life.
_facts: Incorporated August 15, 1887, as one of the first self-governing all-Black municipalities in the United States
Author and anthropologist Zora Neale Hurston (1891-1960) grew up in Eatonville; it features in many of her works including 'Their Eyes Were Watching God'
Robert Hungerford Normal and Industrial School founded 1897 to educate Black students, operated for over 100 years
In 1990 the town founded the Zora Neale Hurston Museum of Fine Arts; annual Zora Festival held each winter
Artist Jules Andre Smith (of the Maitland Art Center) made paintings of Eatonville life in the 1930s-40s

s: Florida's only college teaching museum, home to old master paintings on a Spanish moss campus
w: On the lakeside campus of Rollins College — Florida's oldest private university, founded 1885 by New England Congregationalists — the Rollins Museum of Art (formerly the Cornell Fine Arts Museum) holds 5,000 objects spanning antiquity to today, including rare old master paintings. The only teaching museum in greater Orlando.
_facts: Rollins College founded November 4, 1885 — Florida's fourth oldest post-secondary institution and oldest private university
Established by New England Congregationalists who sought to bring liberal arts education to the St. Johns basin
Museum (formerly Cornell Fine Arts Museum, renamed Rollins Museum of Art) holds 5,000+ objects from antiquity through contemporary eras
The only teaching museum in the greater Orlando area
The Morse Museum (nearby) was first located on Rollins campus and held the first Tiffany exhibition since 1933 in 1955

s: 1935 downtown cinema resurrected as a live-performance stage on Winter Garden's Plant Street
w: Built in 1935 on Plant Street in the heart of old citrus-country Winter Garden, the Garden Theatre sat dark for decades before a community effort brought it back as a live venue in 2008. Its restored marquee anchors one of central Florida's most intact small-town high streets, ringed by the old West Orange citrus belt.
_facts: Built in 1935 on Plant Street in historic downtown Winter Garden, Orange County
Reopened as a performing arts venue in 2008 after community fundraising and restoration
Located in Winter Garden's historic downtown, which developed during the orange-grove boom of the early 20th century
The West Orange Trail, a 22-mile rail-trail on a former railroad right-of-way, passes through Winter Garden downtown
Winter Garden was a major citrus-shipping hub; the railroad brought prosperity and the downtown's brick commercial architecture dates from that era

s: 11-mile drive through a Superfund cleanup that became a bird sanctuary of surpassing richness
w: Once Florida's fourth-largest lake had 21 fish camps; pesticide dumping in 1980 killed the bass and warped the alligators' hormones. The farms were bought out and reflooded as wetland. Now an 11-mile wildlife drive, opened 2014, runs through a landscape of roseate spoonbills and wood storks — redemption in slow motion.
_facts: Lake Apopka was once Central Florida's top bass fishing destination with 21 fish camps on its shores through the 1940s
In July 1980 Tower Chemical Company improperly disposed of DDE and other toxics into the lake; EPA designated it a Superfund site
Pesticide residues caused infertility and hormonal disorders in alligators and bird die-offs; about 676 birds (pelicans, wood storks, herons) died in 1998-99
Lake Apopka Restoration Act (1996) funded farmland purchase; farms were reflooded as wildlife habitat
The Lake Apopka Wildlife Drive (11-mile loop on former farmland levees) opened February 20, 2014

s: 1923 masonry vernacular theater on Sanford's handsome brick Main Street
w: Built in 1923 on Magnolia Avenue in 'Celery City' Sanford, the Ritz has worn several names — Milane, Helen Stairs — before the Wayne Densch bequest fixed its current one. Listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2001. Sanford itself was founded by a Connecticut diplomat who lobbied King Leopold II's Congo claim into US recognition.
_facts: Ritz Theater built 1923 in Masonry Vernacular style at 201 South Magnolia Avenue, Sanford
Also known as Milane Theatre and Helen Stairs Theatre before being renamed after Wayne Densch donations
Added to the US National Register of Historic Places on January 29, 2001
Sanford founded by Henry Shelton Sanford, US Minister to Belgium 1861-69, who also lobbied the US to recognize King Leopold II's claim to the Congo Free State
Downtown Sanford called the 'Historic Waterfront Gateway City'; sits on the southern shore of Lake Monroe at the head of navigation on the St. Johns River

s: Bavarian schnitzel and oompah bands on Florida's most unexpected cobblestone block
w: Theo Hollerbach, a German immigrant, opened this restaurant in downtown Sanford in 2001 and by 2025 it had expanded to anchor a whole Bavarian block. Live oompah bands Thursday through Sunday, imported German beers, and Wiener schnitzel in a city founded by a diplomat who spent his career in Belgium. The incongruity is the entire point.
_facts: Opened by German immigrant Theo Hollerbach in 2001 in historic downtown Sanford
Located at 205 East First Street in Sanford, a downtown area that has reinvented itself as a dining and arts destination
Serves authentic German cuisine — schnitzel, sauerbraten, pretzels — with live German music Thursday through Sunday
Part of a broader Sanford downtown revitalization that has transformed the historic brick streetscape
Sanford sits at head of navigation on the St. Johns River and has a historic marina and waterfront riverwalk

s: One of only four Romanesque Revival courthouses still standing in Florida, built 1889
w: Built in 1889-90 on what is now Courthouse Square, this red-brick Romanesque tower is one of just four such courthouses still standing in Florida. The county's residents voted on its name — options included Kissimmee City, Runnymeade, and 'Hell or Hades.' Kissimmee City won with 421 votes. Chicken theft and moonshine were among the first cases heard.
_facts: Osceola County Courthouse constructed 1889-90 at 3 Courthouse Square, Kissimmee
Romanesque Revival style by architect F.C. Johnson; one of only four remaining examples of this courthouse type in Florida
Added to US National Register of Historic Places August 16, 1977
County name election held February 6, 1887; options were Kissimmee City, Runnymeade, and 'Hell or Hades'; Kissimmee City won 421 votes
First county bonds issued July 1, 1889 at 7% interest; final courthouse payment made in July 1909

s: Disney's utopian new-urbanist town, designed by Philip Johnson and Michael Graves
w: Disney broke ground in 1994 on a 4,900-acre master-planned community where the post office was designed by Michael Graves, the Welcome Center by Philip Johnson, and the SunTrust Bank by Robert Venturi. CEO Michael Eisner urged executives to 'make history.' Disney quietly sold the town off in 2004. The leaky roofs and mold came later.
_facts: Developed from 1994 by the Disney Development Company on 4,900 acres of the Reedy Creek Improvement District
Downtown buildings designed by star architects: Michael Graves (post office), Philip Johnson (Welcome Center), Robert Venturi and Denise Scott Brown (SunTrust Bank), Cesar Pelli (movie theater)
Master plan by Jacquelin T. Robertson and Robert A.M. Stern; named 'New Community of the Year' 2001 by the Urban Land Institute
Michael Eisner encouraged executives to 'make history' and build a town worthy of Walt Disney's EPCOT vision
Disney sold commercial assets to Lexin Capital in 2004; a 2016 Wall Street Journal report revealed widespread building decay and mold lawsuits
